figures.caption: Figure 1: Three independent samples from the conditional SPDE-based GMRF prior p(x|δ=1) on the three-dimensional eccentric hyper-shell domain (see Section 3.1 for the geometry). Samples are obtained by solving A∗ L x = η with η ∼ N (0,M), where A∗ L = AL + κ2 M is the SPDE operator (Equation (4), κ2 = 10−4), yielding the SPDE covariance Cov(x|δ) = δ−1 (A∗ L) −1 M (A∗ L) −1. The colorbar indicates the value of the individual field samples x̃i(c).

Figure 2: Computational workflow of the adjoint model A(x), which returns the full log-posterior gradient ∇x log p(xi,yobs). Steps 1–3 solve the forward problem, compute the partial likelihood gradient, and solve the adjoint system for λi. Step 4 assembles the log-posterior gradient: the likelihood contribution∇xL(x) (Equation (31)) via element-wise contraction of λi with ∇xd, and the prior gradient via the SPDE precision A∗ L M−1 A∗ L, computed through two sparse products with A∗ L and one mass-matrix solve, reusing matrices already assembled in the FE solver.

Table 1: Overview of posterior covariance approximations. Methods are characterized by their treatment of the forward model, the basis used for covariance truncation, the resulting covariance structure, and the memory cost in terms of the stochastic field dimension ndofs, with r the retained rank (low-rank Laplace) and J the ensemble size (EKI).

Figure 3: Spherical computational domain Ω (center P2 = [0, 0, 0] and radius r2 = 1.0) with an eccentric spherical void (center P1 = [0, 0.1, 0.25] and radius r1 = 0.3), meshed with hexahedral elements. Left: Slice through the c2 − c3 plane, which contains the centers P1 and P2. The outer surface of the domain Ω is denoted by Γp, on which we prescribe the pressure boundary p(c) = b(c) = 0. The inner surface of Ω (surface of the void) is denoted by Γu on which we prescribe a no-penetration velocity boundary condition u(c) · nΓu(c) = w(c) = 0. Right: 3D view of the domain with the void.
Table 2: Overview of employed FEM discretization and settings.

Figure 4: Three-dimensional ground-truth permeability field kgt(c) (scalar field). Top row: Different 3D visualizations with slices through the origin at (0, 0, 0) along with a slightly transparent iso-surface (beige color) for kgt = 2.5. Bottom row: Slices through the domain and origin (0, 0, 0). From left to right: c2 − c3-plane, c1 − c3-plane, and c1 − c2-plane. The inclusion of Ω∗ is clearly visible as a beige cube.

Figure 5: 3D representation of the resulting ground-truth velocity field ugt(c): We afterwards generate the observables yobs by evaluating the velocity at points defined by a coarser triangulation of the same geometry and corrupting the resulting values with i. i. d. Gaussian noise with an SNR of 20 (not shown here).

Figure 6: Noise-polluted velocity observations, depicted as magnitude-scaled vector field. Left: Image of all noisy observation vectors. Right: Rescaled and thinned out representation for a better impression of the noise level.
Table 3: Bayesian inverse problem setup for the high-dimensional 3D porous media flow example.

Figure 7: Posterior mean of the reconstructed permeability field Eq(x|ϕ∗) [k(x, c)] (scalar field). Top row: 3D visualizations with slices through the origin at (0, 0, 0). Bottom row: Slices through the domain and origin (0, 0, 0). From left to right: c2-c3-plane, c1-c3-plane, and c1-c2-plane. Compare with the ground-truth field in Figure 4.

Figure 8: Two posterior standard deviations 2 STDq(x|ϕ∗)[k(x, c)] of the reconstructed permeability field (scalar field). Regions with high uncertainty indicate areas where the observational data provide limited information about the permeability. Top row: 3D visualizations with slices through the origin at (0, 0, 0). Bottom row: Slices through the domain and origin (0, 0, 0). From left to right: c2-c3-plane, c1-c3-plane, and c1-c2-plane.

Figure 9: Algorithmic ablation: convergence diagnostics for the ten configurations that each modify a single internal axis of Algorithm 1 relative to the baseline. (a) posterior-mean discrepancy εpm = ∥µ− xgt∥2/∥xgt∥2; (b) relative predictive residual εpred = ∥ȳ − yobs∥2/∥yobs∥2; (c) effective likelihood precision τ̃ (log scale); (d) effective prior precision δ̃ (log scale). Corresponding converged numbers are reported in the algorithmic-ablation rows of Table 4.

Figure 10: Comparison with alternative inference methods: convergence diagnostics for the proposed sparse-precision SVI against three alternative inference methods (mean-field SVI, sparse banded-covariance SVI with off-diagonal bandwidth 10, and the Laplace approximation), run on the same forward problem, GMRF prior, and adjoint pipeline under otherwise identical settings. The Laplace baseline is realized within the present framework by collapsing the variational covariance to a near-Dirac diagonal, replacing the VB-EM update on δ with MacKay’s evidence approximation [98], and disabling natural-gradient preconditioning (which requires a non-degenerate variational covariance); see the corresponding paragraph in Section 3.4 for details. (a) posterior-mean discrepancy εpm = ∥µ−xgt∥2/∥xgt∥2; (b) relative predictive residual εpred = ∥ȳ− yobs∥2/∥yobs∥2; (c) effective likelihood precision τ̃ (log scale); (d) effective prior precision δ̃ (log scale).

Table 4: Ablation and comparison study: converged diagnostics after 500 SVI iterations (dim(x) = 405 570). The table is divided into two blocks: (i) Algorithmic ablation, configurations that each modify a single internal component of Algorithm 1 relative to the baseline; and (ii) Comparison with alternative inference methods, all sharing the proposed prior, data, and adjoint pipeline (the proposed sparse-precision SVI is the baseline of block (i) and is not duplicated here). Within block (i), rows are sorted by εpm after the pinned baseline; the two trailing rows correspond to prior-basin failures, in which the variational mean does not leave the prior basin. Bold marks the baseline and the smallest value in each error column of that block. The third column reports the effective likelihood precision τ̃ (posterior mean of τ under the Gamma hyperprior); the corresponding δ̃ trajectories are in panel (d) of Figure 9 for block (i) and Figure 10 for block (ii).

Figure 11: Independent posterior samples drawn from q(x|ϕ∗), visualized as 3D isosurfaces of the permeability field k(x, c). Each row corresponds to one sample shown from three viewpoints (same as Figure 4). All samples capture the dominant spatial structure of the ground truth, while fine-scale variations across samples reflect the remaining posterior uncertainty. Compare with the posterior mean in Figure 7 and the ground truth in Figure 4.
